coredata.dc:title: Poly(vinyl alcohol)/nanocellulose film integrated with phenolic waste-based carbon dots for ultraviolet-blocking and flame retardant applications
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Longfei
Peng, Limin
Liang, Shanqing
Xie, Xinfeng
Lyu, Shaoyi
Wang, Siqun
coredata.dc:description: Recycling of thermosetting plastic waste and development of functional biopolymer-based films have become captivating issues in the worldwide recently. The current work highlights the facile approach to transform the thermosetting phenolic foam (PF) waste to dual-functional carbon dots (CDs) via simple hydrothermal treatment. The obtained CDs have displayed good graphite structure with rich carbon (C), nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), and phosphorus (P) elements. The absorption peak and quantum yield (QY) values of N, S, P-doped CDs (N,S,P-CD PF ) were affected by the hydrothermal precursors and reaction. The fluorescent N,S,P-CD PF derived from PF have further been assembled with the poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) and oxidized nanofibrillated cellulose (ONFC) to develop ultraviolet (UV) blocking and flame retardant (FR) films via the solution casting approach. The morphology, transparency, UV–visible behavior, and flame retardancy of the composite films were studied. The light-blocking efficiency and flame retardancy of the films were verified by adjusting the loading ratio of N,S,P-CD PF in the films. The film with 0.05 wt% N,S,P-CD PF can absorb most of UV and some blue light (∼39.6 % at 450 nm), and retain 76.5 % transparency at 800 nm. In addition, the PF-derived N,S,P-CD PF have proved to be good flame retardant owing to the increased limiting oxygen index (LOI) >31.7 %, and reduced heat-release rate (HRR) from 287.4 W/g to 127.2 W/g for the PVA-ONFC@CD PF5 film compared to the pristine PVA film. Finally, the current studies provide a promising strategy for recycling of PF waste as a flame retardant UV absorber and create awareness regarding the flame retardant and flexible UV-blocking packaging materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Flame retardant
Composite film
Recycling

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs with recycled PF plastic waste
Formation of PVA/ONFC/N,S,P-CD PF composite films
UV and HEBL blocking activity of PVA/ONFC/N,S,P-CD PF films
Flame retardant properties of PVA/ONFC/N,S,P-CD PF films
Morphology and structure of fluorescent N,S,P-CD PF
Characterization of PVA/ONFC/N,S,P-CD PF composite films
UV-blocking and mechanical behaviors of composite films
Flame retardant properties of composite films

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic for the production of CDs from PF waste and the resulted dual-functional composite films for UV-blocking and flame retardant application.

(a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of N,S,P-CD PF . Inset is the lattice fringes; (c) Particle size distribution of dots; (d) 2D-AFM and (e) 3D-AFM image of dots; (f) AFM height profiles of two random dots; (g) FTIR spectra and (h) XRD pattern of dots.
XPS spectra of the PF-derived N,S,P-CD PF . (a) XPS score, (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s, (d) N 1s, (e) S 2p, and (f) P 2p spectra.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of N,S,P-CD PF solution; (b) PL spectra of N,S,P-CD PF solution in 3D mode at λex (300–445 nm); (c) Control water and solution with dots ratios (0.01, 0.05, and 0.10 wt%) exposed to daylight (up) and 365 nm UV light (down); (d) PL emission of N,S,P-CD PF solution (0.05 wt%) in 2D mapping mode. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) FTIR spectra of films; (b) FTIR spectra of PF waste and PF residuals; Digital pictures of films with various wt% of dots (c) under daylight, and (d) under UV-lamp; (e) Flexibility of the film with 0.10 wt% dots; SEM image of (f) PVA, (g) PVA-ONFC, and (h) PVA-ONFC@CD PF10 films. Inset is the cross-sectional image.

PL spectra in (a) 3D and (b) 2D mapping of the PVA-ONFC@CD PF5 (0.05 wt%) film; (c) PL spectra of PVA-ONFC@CD PF5 film with different λex; (d) Decay lifetime of three films; (e) Mechanical property of films with various ratios of dots; (f) Schematic of H-bonding in the film.

(a) UV–vis absorbance and (b) transmittance spectra of films; (c) UV-blocking against UV-LED (380 nm). Inset is the PL emission of films; UV and HEBL blocking under (d) sunlight and (e) indoor CFL-lamp. Inset is the magnified curve; (f) HEBL blocking against a smartphone (Inset shows the screen with our transparent films).
Schematic for the mechanism of N,S,P-CD PF in the UV-blocking film.
(a) TGA and (b) HRR curves of different PVA-based films; Snapshots of UL-94 test of (c) PVA, (d) PVA/ONFC, (e) PVA-ONFC@CD PF1 , and (f) PVA-ONFC@CD PF5 composite films.
LOI, UL-94, and MCC heat release results for different PVA-based composite films.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Afterflame time means the burning time of the film specimens after removing the igniter (s).

head.ce:title: Poly(vinyl alcohol)/nanocellulose film integrated with phenolic waste-based carbon dots for ultraviolet-blocking and flame retardant applications

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: S. Lyu, Research Institute of Wood Industry, Chinese Academy of Forestry, Beijing 100091, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recycling of thermosetting plastic waste and development of functional biopolymer-based films have become captivating issues in the worldwide recently. The current work highlights the facile approach to transform the thermosetting phenolic foam (PF) waste to dual-functional carbon dots (CDs) via simple hydrothermal treatment. The obtained CDs have displayed good graphite structure with rich carbon (C), nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), and phosphorus (P) elements. The absorption peak and quantum yield (QY) values of N, S, P-doped CDs (N,S,P-CD PF ) were affected by the hydrothermal precursors and reaction. The fluorescent N,S,P-CD PF derived from PF have further been assembled with the poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) and oxidized nanofibrillated cellulose (ONFC) to develop ultraviolet (UV) blocking and flame retardant (FR) films via the solution casting approach. The morphology, transparency, UV–visible behavior, and flame retardancy of the composite films were studied. The light-blocking efficiency and flame retardancy of the films were verified by adjusting the loading ratio of N,S,P-CD PF in the films. The film with 0.05 wt% N,S,P-CD PF can absorb most of UV and some blue light (∼39.6 % at 450 nm), and retain 76.5 % transparency at 800 nm. In addition, the PF-derived N,S,P-CD PF have proved to be good flame retardant owing to the increased limiting oxygen index (LOI) >31.7 %, and reduced heat-release rate (HRR) from 287.4 W/g to 127.2 W/g for the PVA-ONFC@CD PF5 film compared to the pristine PVA film. Finally, the current studies provide a promising strategy for recycling of PF waste as a flame retardant UV absorber and create awareness regarding the flame retardant and flexible UV-blocking packaging materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, human exposure to electronic devices such as compact fluorescent lights (CFLs), smartphones, tablets, and light-emitting diodes (LEDs) is inevitable [1]. Therefore, it is profitable to develop Ultraviolet (UV)-blocking materials via the integration of UV absorbers to meet practical applications. Generally, the spectral range of unhealthy light primarily comprises wavelengths at 100–450 nm; however, it is typically classified as UV-C (100–280 nm), UV-B (280–315 nm), UV-A (315–400 nm), and high-energy blue light (HEBL; 400–450 nm) [2,3]. Most of UV and HEBL from sunlight and electronic products are harmful to eyes, fruits, and other polymers. The traditional UV absorbers are some inorganic particles (e.g., TiO2, SiO2, and ZnO) and graphene nanoparticles [4–6]. Although the majority of traditional UV absorbers presenting good UV-blocking behaviors, most mental-based nanoparticles imply low flame retardancy and potential catalytic activities to degrade the polymeric matrices [7].

Poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) is a promising coating material owing to its high transparency, film-forming ability, and biodegradable behaviors [8,9]. It is widely used in flexible fibers, wearable devices, adhesives, shielding, and packaging materials [10,11]. Unfortunately, pristine PVA has poor mechanical and flame retardant properties, indicating that it is brittle and easily ignitable. Moreover, PVA generally ignites rapidly with substantial melt-droplets, which is prone to aggravate the flame spreading [12]. These defects have prevented the practical application of PVA films in high-strength and fire-proofing fields. According to previous reports [13,14], PVA films containing very low content of nanocellulose (∼1.0 wt%) can maintain good transparency and high mechanical properties owing to its competitive reinforcing effect in composites. Therefore, UV-blocking films with natural PVA/nanocellulose matrix should be developed to shield these harmful lights while retaining good visibility, high strength, and flame retardant properties.

To address the UV-blocking and flammability of PVA-based composites, the convenient solutions include integration with UV-absorber and flame retardants (FRs) [15,16]. However, the inorganic hydroxides usually require high addition content (generally ≥50 %), which results in low transparency and weak mechanical properties with brittleness [17]. Recently, an increasing number of studies have evaluated the N-, S-, P-, and N/S/P-containing flame retardants because they are prone to form a dense carbon layer in combustion process, thus reducing the flammability [18].

In recent years, carbon-based nanomaterials have been proposed as UV-blocking absorber and flame retardants. Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted much attention as a novel fluorescent carbon material (usually <10 nm). Owing to their good photoluminescence (PL), CDs have been applied in the catalysis, imaging [19], sensing [20], bacterial resistant [21], and UV-shielding fields [22,23]. However, the majority of these CDs were based on thermally-treated biomass such as wood, grass, and fruits. Chaudhary et al. [24] have also showed the approach to transform the thermoplastic waste to CDs via a heating process. These biomass and thermoplastic waste-based CDs with only C- and O-containing structure perform bad flame retardancy. However, limited reports are available towards CDs prepared from thermosetting polymers.

Thermosetting polymers generally presented excellent heat resistance. Every year, numerous polymers have been disposed of as waste, which takes hundreds or even thousands of years to decompose [25–27]. Phenolic foam (PF) is a typical thermosetting polymer with excellent properties, such as low-density and flame resistance [28,29]. It was prepared by blending phenol-formaldehyde (or lignin-modified resin) with strong acid-catalyst agents (such as phosphoric acid, sulfuric acid, p-toluene sulfonic acid, and other acid mixtures) and plentiful additives [30]. In traditional factories, PF waste are transformed into finer particles and reused as fillers [29,31]. However, these methods do not fully utilize the valuable additives in PF [32]. Recently, hydrothermal carbonization strategy has been proven to be a facile method to develop functional CDs with different precursors [33]. Therefore, it is urgent to utilize the useful compounds (C-, N-, P-, and S-) in the PF waste and develop useful CDs with excellent UV-blocking behavior and flame retardant properties.

In this work, PF thermosetting polymer waste was recycled to synthesize fluorescent N-, S-, and P-doped CDs (N,S,P-CD PF ) by using a facile hydrothermal treatment. Moreover, novel composite films composed of N,S,P-CD PF , PVA, and nanocellulose are demonstrated. The results show that PVA/nanocellulose composite films containing N,S,P-CD PF have improved tensile strength, flame retardancy, UV-blocking properties, and are highly transparent in the visible spectra. Furthermore, these flame retardant films can absorb harmful UV lights and down-convert them into low-energy lights, leading to good flame retardant and UV-blocking materials.

Typical PF (1#, fabrication via phenols and additives) and lignin-modified PF (LPF) (2#, fabrication via modified-phenols and additives) with a density of 50–60 kg/m3 were recycled from the insulation material market (Beijing, China). Table S1 lists the basic properties of the PF samples. The limiting oxygen index (LOI), residual phenol, and formaldehyde were obtained using the ISO 4589-2-2006, ISO 8974-2002, and ASTM D2863–17. The oxidized nanofibrillated cellulose (ONFC) with a carboxyl substitution degree of 0.42 was purchased from Haojia Ltd. (Tianjin, China). PVA 1750 and citric acid (CA) were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Deionized water was used as a dispersing solvent for the all sample preparation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs with recycled PF plastic waste
ce:section.ce:para: The PF, LPF, and PF/LPF mixture were crushed to obtain a fine powder. A yellow mixture (4.5 g) and CA (0.12 g) were mixed in deionized water (300 mL) via magnetic stirring. The suspension was then transferred to a 500-mL container and heated at 220 °C for 6 h for CDs. The resulting mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 8 min to eliminate large particles. Furthermore, dialysis in water using 3.5 kDa osmosis bags was essential to remove unreacted acids. The solution in the bags was dried in a freeze dryer; subsequently, a solution with a mass ratio of 2.0 wt% was prepared for further film formation. For comparison, pristine PF, LPF, and PF/LPF (mass ratio of 1:1) were hydrothermally processed in water without additional chemicals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation of PVA/ONFC/N,S,P-CD PF composite films
ce:section.ce:para: The schematic for the production of CDs from the PF waste and the dual-functional films for UV-blocking and flame retardant film is shown in Fig. 1 . To embed N,S,P-CD PF in a PVA/ONFC matrix, the PVA was dissolved in water at 90 °C for 3 h to prepare a PVA solution with a mass ratio of 7.5 wt%. According to previous reports [13,14], the ONFC was added to the PVA solution with a solid-to-solid ratio of 1:100. Different ratios of dots were mixed with the suspension. Subsequently, the dispersion was casted onto dishes and then dried at 55 °C for 24 h to obtain films. The film thicknesses were maintained within 85–100 μm. The films with different ratios of N,S,P-CD PF (0, 0.01, 0.05, and 0.10 wt%) were named PVA-ONFC, PVA-ONFC@CD PF1 , PVA-ONFC@CD PF5 , and PVA-ONFC@CD PF10 , respectively.

The microstructure of CDs was observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM; FEI G2-F30, USA) operating at 200 kV. Morphologies of CDs were investigated using an atomic force microscope (AFM; Bruker, Germany). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using an XRD meter (Rigaku, Japan) at a rate of 4°/min. The elemental composition of dots was analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Thermo, USA). Elemental analysis of N,S,P-CD PF was conducted using an Elemental CHN analyzer (Vario cube, Germany). Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using a Nicolet IS10 spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). The absorbance spectra were measured using a GD-54T UV–visible (UV–vis) spectrometer (Lengguang, China). Fluorescence PL spectra in the three-dimensional (3D) and 2D modes were obtained using an FLS100/FS5 spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). The relative fluorescence QY of the CDs was calculated according to our published study [20], and quinine sulfate (0.10 M H2SO4 as solvent) was used as the standard with a QY of 0.54 at 360 nm. Zeta potential values were determined using a Nano ZS Zetasizer (Malvern, UK). The morphologies were examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM; Hitachi SU8020, Japan). Tensile tests were performed using a testing system (MTS-E43, USA). The films were cut into strips with a narrow width of 5 mm. A strain rate of 5 mm/min was employed for each tensile test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV and HEBL blocking activity of PVA/ONFC/N,S,P-CD PF films
ce:section.ce:para: The UV and HEBL blocking behaviors of different films with N,S,P-CD PF were estimated using the UV–vis spectra. The light blocking behaviors of the films were estimated using the integral calculation [34]. The calculation equations are shown in Table S2. Four typical optical sources were applied owing to their close relation to life: sunlight (116°20′ E, 39°56′ N), CFLs, LEDs (370 nm), and smartphones. The blocking spectra were recorded using films fixed between optical sources and an optical fiber spectrometer (S5000, Hamamatsu Photonics).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flame retardant properties of PVA/ONFC/N,S,P-CD PF films
ce:section.ce:para: The flame retardancy was studied by evaluating the LOI, micro-scale heat release, and vertical combustion (UL-94) performances. The thermal stability was evaluated using an STA 449 F5/F3 thermo-gravimetric analyzer (TGA; NETZSCH, Germany). The samples were heated from 40 °C to 800 °C at 10 °C/min under nitrogen. The micro-scale heat release was evaluated using a MCC-2 micro-calorimeter (MCC, Govmark, USA) according to the ASTM D7309-19. Approximately 5 mg of the film sample was heated from room temperature to 750 °C at a heating rate of 1 °C/s under nitrogen. The LOI values were measured on HC-2CZ oxygen index meter (Nanjing, China). The film dimension of LOI and vertical combustion experiments is 200 × 50 × (∼0.10) mm3 according to ASTM D2863-17 and ASTM D4804-14, respectively. The combustion snapshots were captured using an IR imaging camera (Ti100, Fluke, USA).

The successful synthesis of N,S,P-CD PF was implemented by utilizing the affluent matter in PF waste as precursors via a one-step hydrothermal route. PF waste containing residual phenol, lignin, free strong acids (such as H3PO4, H2SO4, and other acids), and other additives. These matters are easily available for large-scale synthesis of doped CDs, the optical properties of which may be altered by adjusting the precursor. In this study, PF, LPF, PF/LPF (mass ratio of 1:1), and PF/LPF/CA (mass ratio of 1:1:0.05) were used to fabricate fluorescent dots.

The PL behavior of the dots depended on the composition of the PF waste. The QY and decay lifetimes are important for determining the activity of N,S,P-CD PF . In our preliminary experiments, the effects of different reaction temperatures and times on the QY of the CDs were investigated. As revealed in Fig. S1, the optimum group for N,S,P-CD PF with a high QY (12.6 %) was PF/LPF with a very low CA (mass ratio of 1:0.025) compared to that of the PF/LPF (QY = 8.9 %), and the optimum reaction condition was 220 °C and 6 h. Moreover, to verify the PL mechanisms of the PF/LPF/CA systems, the lifetimes of N,S,P-CD PF from PF/LPF in the absence and presence of CA were measured (Fig. S2). According to the best-fitting data, the PL lifetime can be fitted well. The average lifetime of the dots from the PF/LPF/CA precursors was determined to be 6.24 ns, which is higher than that of the PF/LPF (3.85 ns). This change may be attributed to the higher ratio of carbon-cores owing to the incorporation of CA, thereby affecting the electron-transfer process of CDs. Because the PF/LPF/CA strategy is easy to access and the dots possess a relatively high QY and lifetime, they were chosen as the representative CDs for further film formation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure of fluorescent N,S,P-CD PF
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2a, the TEM image of N,S,P-CD PF reveals the formation of localized clusters of spots. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of dot in Fig. 2b further clearly displays a graphitic crystal structure with lattice fringes of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (1120) crystal planes of graphite structures [35,36]. From the particle size distributions of N,S,P-CD PF (Fig. 2c), these clusters are nearly spherical with an average diameter of (4.41 ± 0.11) nm. The AFM images (Fig. 2d and e) also indicate the formation of well-dispersed particles and some aggregations. Although the N,S,P-CD PF aggregate owing to strong H-bonding, a single dot height profile was still identified as 7 nm and ∼10 nm from the AFM height profiles of two dots (Fig. 2f). The elemental analysis, presented in Table S3a, shows that N,S,P-CD PF was composed of C 33.68, H 7.06, N 4.87, S 4.61, P 10.52, and O (calculated) 38.26 wt%. The relative atom numbers of dots were calculated and are listed in Table S3b. The estimated formula for a single dot is C39H98N5S2P5O33, which indicates that N, S, and P atoms were assembled to form multi-functional groups.

The FTIR spectra reveal that the N,S,P-CD PF was highly functionalized with various groups (Fig. 2g). The broad peak at 3247 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of the O−H or N−H bonds. The strong peak at 2938 cm−1 is attributed to C−H vibrations. The four sharp peaks at 1712, 1543, 1401, and 1353 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO, CN, COOH groups, and C−N/C−S, respectively. Other peaks corresponding to P−O bending (1172 cm−1), −SO3 (1006 cm−1), and P−OH (884 cm−1) have also been observed [37]. The peak at 495 cm−1 is attributed to the vibration of phosphates [38]. These results imply amino, sulfuric, and phosphate groups on the surface of N,S,P-CD PF . The XRD patterns of N,S,P-CD PF in Fig. 2h show a broad diffraction peak at 2θ values of 18–33°, which can be assigned to the (002) lattice planes and disordered structures of graphitic carbon [39,40]. Moreover, the small peaks at 25.8°, 36.1°, and 41.9° can be attributed to a few modified graphitic carbon in ordered modes. These results indicate that the dots consist of crystalline and amorphous particles.

XPS was performed to further determine the oxidation state of the N,S,P-CD PF . The full-scan XPS profile (Fig. 3a) reveals that the dots are composed of O, N, C, S, and P atoms. The atomic contents are consistent with the elemental analysis results (Table S3). The C1s spectrum in Fig. 3b displays chemical bonds at 289.0, 287.6, 286.8, 286.0, 285.3, and 284.6 eV, ascribed to the COOH, CO, C=N/C=P, C−N/C−S, C−OH/C−O−C, and C−C/C−P, respectively [41,42]. The resolved peaks in the O 1s spectrum at 533.1, 532.4, and 531.5 eV are assigned to the PO, C−OH/C−O−C, and COOH, respectively (Fig. 3c). Two peaks in the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 3d) occur at 401.9 and 399.9 eV, corresponding to pyrrolic-N and amino-N, respectively [43]. The enlarged S 2p spectrum (Fig. 3e) also shows three peaks at 169.8, 168.8, and 168.0 eV, ascribed to the −C−SOx, SO, and C−S/N−S, respectively. In addition, the 134.9 and 133.9 eV peaks in the P 2p spectrum (Fig. 3f) indicate the presence of the P−C and P−S/P−O groups [44]. These results were consistent with the FTIR result. Fig. S3 illustrates that the zeta potential of the N,S,P-CD PF solution was −27.5 mV, and it is beneficial for the H-bonds between the N,S,P-CD PF and PVA/ONFC matrices to form good films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of PVA/ONFC/N,S,P-CD PF composite films
ce:section.ce:para: The optical and PL properties of the N,S,P-CD PF solutions are shown in Fig. 4 . As shown in Fig. 4a, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the N,S,P-CD PF exhibits strong absorption at 340 nm and a weak shoulder peak at 248 nm. The peak at 340 nm matches the n − π* transitions of CO and C−N bonds, whereas the shoulder absorption at 248 nm arises from the π − π* transitions of aromatic CC bonds [45]. The UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 4a) of these dots also exhibits broad tailing at 370–460 nm, which helps absorb additional HEBL (400–450 nm) owing to the oxygen-related groups and surface defects in the dots. Moreover, N,S,P-CD PF solution exhibited λex-dependent PL behaviors with fluctuating λex and emitted blue to blue–green light (Fig. 4b). Owing to the strong absorption behavior of UV, these dots have the potential for UV-shielding applications.

The aqueous dispersions under 365 nm UV light exhibited strong blue luminescence (Fig. 4c), which enhanced as the ratio of dots increased from 0.01 to 0.05 wt%; however, this was not obvious as the ratio increased from 0.05 to 0.10 wt%. Furthermore, the 2D mapping of the N,S,P-CD PF emission shows that these dots emitted blue light (∼450 nm) when excited by UV light and emitted blue–green light (480–500 nm) when λex was 400–450 nm (Fig. 4d). The N,S,P-CD PF had a compatible QY (12.6 %) when excited at 360 nm and emitted weak blue light. It could reabsorb blue lights (400–450 nm) and convert them into blue–green light. Consequently, the PL behavior of N,S,P-CD PF was responsible for UV-blocking application owing to the broad UV absorption and their abilities to down-convert harmful light into lower-energy (longer-wavelength) visible light.

The FTIR spectra of the PVA-ONFC and PVA-ONFC@CD PF5 films are shown in Fig. 5a. Compared to the PVA film, two pronounced peaks at 1602 and 1005 cm−1 were observed for the PVA-ONFC, which are the carboxylate groups (–COO−Na+) overlapping with the OH and the vibration mode of –SO3 [46]. For the PVA-ONFC@CD PF5 film, the typical peaks of N,S,P-CD PF (1186 and 1005 cm−1), PVA (1094 cm−1), and ONFC (1602 and 1005 cm−1) appeared in the film (Fig. 5a). Moreover, the O−H stretching of PVA slightly decreased and shifted to 3252 cm−1 after the integration of dots, whereas the O−H bending of bound water at 1658 cm−1 for PVA shifted to 1602 cm−1 in the luminescent films. In general, the −OH can be associated or disassociation, which can affect the O−H bonding [47]. Thus, the FTIR results indicate the formation of H-bonds in the films.

To demonstrate the effect of pyrolysis on the structure of PF plastic, Fig. 5b shows the FTIR spectra of the PF residuals after the hydrothermal reaction. The treatment had no adverse effect on the aromatic skeleton (1632 and 1485 cm−1) and bridge (–CH2) of PF. However, the ether bond (C–O–C) at 1128 cm−1 in the PF residuals was broken, and the signal of the primary alcohol (1032 cm−1) decreased. Moreover, the signal of phenolic hydroxyl groups at 1205 cm−1 increased, and many new derivatives were produced owing to the signals at 907, 819, and 698 cm−1. Therefore, the dots are likely to be derived from aromatic fractures, additives, and residual acids in PF. Good transparency of the film is essential for its light-related field, and photographs of the films are shown in Fig. 5c and d. Films with various ratios of dots are highly transparent owing to the visible logo beneath the films. Furthermore, the uniformity of the dots in the film was examined using a 380-nm UV-lamp (Fig. 5d). The films exhibited amplifying PL with increasing ratio of dots. When the ratio of dots was higher than 0.05 wt%, the PL behavior was not noticeable. Additionally, the PVA-ONFC film with 0.10 wt% N,S,P-CD PF also presented good flexibility. The SEM images of the composite films are shown in Fig. 5f–h. These films had smooth surfaces. The inset images of PVA-ONFC (Fig. 5g) and PVA-ONFC@CD PF10 (Fig. 5h) appeared to be wavier than that of the PVA film (Fig. 5f), indicating that the ONFC (fibrils) and N,S,P-CD PF were uniformly dispersed in the PVA. The thickness of the film was ∼85 μm, and irregular fibril structures were observed in the cross-section (Fig. 5h). Importantly, the formation of a flat film also helps prevent the quenching of agglomerated dots because of the H-bonding in PVA/ONFC composite films.

The photo-induced activity of the composite films was evaluated by their PL spectra, which also helped to explain the wavelength-converting behavior in absorbing UV radiation. Fig. 6a and b show the 3D and 2D mapping spectra of PVA-ONFC@CD PF5 film with different excitations. The PL behavior of N,S,P-CD PF in PVA/ONFC matrix is similar to that of N,S,P-CD PF in dispersions. As λex increased from 300 to 450 nm, the PL emission of the films shows evident changes. Moreover, the emission of the other two films with different ratios of N,S,P-CD PF also exhibited similar excitation-reliant activity. Fig. 6c displays the normalized PL spectra with λex at 300, 350, 400, and 450 nm. When λex was 300 and 350 nm, a similar wide spectrum was observed, with peaks at 438 and 442 nm, respectively. This proves that UV can be converted to longer-wavelength emissions. Furthermore, as λex increases to 400 and 450 nm (HEBL), the PL emission exhibits a wider half-wavelength spectrum with peaks at 475 nm and 505 nm, respectively, indicating that harmful HEBL can also be transferred to blue–green lights. This activity is mostly owing to the electronic transitions from oxidized groups (primarily CO, PO, and −COOH) in dots [48]. The PL spectra of the films indicate that they can convert and emit blue and blue–green lights upon UV and HEBL excitations. The PL decay lifetime curves of the PVA-ONFC@CD PF films are displayed in Fig. 6d. The lifetime was well fitted using a double-exponential function (Fig. S4). The average lifetimes (τ avg) of the PVA-ONFC@CD PF films range from 5.74 to 4.25 ns. These τ avg values of the blocking films displayed a slight decrease with increasing ratio of dots. This is attributed to the electronic transfer between closely embedded N,S,P-CD PF with limited PVA/ONFC confinement effect, thereby decreasing the lifetime and further facilitating the PL emission at longer-wavelengths.

Good mechanical behavior is also essential for the application of composite films. The tensile results for the films are shown in Fig. 6e and f. Compared to the PVA film, the tensile strength (55.8 MPa) and elongation at break (140.7 %) of the PVA-ONFC@CD PF films were significantly enhanced with increasing N,S,P-CD PF content (≤0.05 wt%). Furthermore, these values are ∼2 and ∼3 times than those of the PVA film, respectively. This is ascribed to the good dispersion of ONFC (fibrils) and dots, and the formation of H-bonding, resulting in a denser film surface [49]. Fig. 6f shows a schematic of the H-bonding structure between oxidized groups on N,S,P-CD PF and –OH groups in PVA/ONFC polymers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV-blocking and mechanical behaviors of composite films
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the applications of the films in the UV and HEBL blocking fields, the spectra of the N,S,P-CD PF -loaded PVA-ONFC@CD PF films are summarized in Fig. 7a and b. The PVA and PVA-ONFC films had the lowest absorbance in 200–800 nm and were highly transparent in the UV-A and visible regions (>88.5 %). However, the control PVA-ONFC film could only block 15.5 % of UV-B (300 nm) and 13.7 % of UV-A light (350 nm), indicating that the PVA-ONFC films are unsuitable for blocking harmful UV light. For the PVA-ONFC@CD PF films (Fig. 7a and b), the light absorbance increased with higher content of dots, whereas the transmittance consequently decreased, particularly in the UV region (<400 nm). This is ascribed to the strong UV absorption of the dots. Moreover, the films with dots remained highly transparent at 450–800 nm. This is because that N,S,P-CD PF rarely absorbs visible light in this region owing to the minimal PL efficiency.

Figs. S5 and S6 present the visible light (460 and 800 nm) transparencies of films compared with the UV blocking behaviors. As the N,S,P-CD PF loading ratios increased from 0.01 to 0.10 wt%, most of UV-A and UV-B radiations were shielded, whereas the transparency remained high at 800 nm (>61.2 %). The 0.05 wt% N,S,P-CD PF film blocked ∼89.4 % of UV-A, ∼75.8 % of UV-B, and 35.4 % of visible light at 460 nm (Fig. S5). However, the PVA-ONFC@CD PF10 film effectively blocked UV-A and ∼50.8 % of HEBL at 450 nm, along with 61.2 % transparency at 800 nm (Fig. S6). Therefore, these results (Table S4) confirm that PVA/ONFC composite films with N,S,P-CD PF can exhibit good visible light transparency and shield most of UV lights.

Based on the PVA-ONFC@CD PF films, which can block most of UV and sort of HEBL radiation, various optical sources such as UV-LEDs, sunlight, CFLs, and smartphones, were selected to verify the good light-blocking behaviors of the proposed films (Fig. 7c–d). Fig. 8 show the schematic for the mechanism of PF-derived N,S,P-CD PF in the application of UV-blocking film. Fig. 7c presents the efficient UV-shielding behavior of the PVA-ONFC@CD PF films and their visible light emissions. The UV-LED emitted strongly and presented a sharp emission peak at 380 nm, whereas the UV radiation was reduced to lower levels for the PVA-ONFC@CD PF films, depending on the different ratios of dots. In this study, the UV and HEBL intensities effectively declined when sunlight was passed through the films (Fig. 7d). In particular, the film with 0.10 wt% of N,S,P-CD PF could efficiently shield UV radiation and sort of HEBL from the sunlight spectrum. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 7e and f, these films also block the type of HEBL radiation from indoor CFLs and the electronic screens of smartphones. This indicates the potential application of these films in strong UV-shielding and weak HEBL-shielding fields.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flame retardant properties of composite films
ce:section.ce:para: The flame retardant properties of pristine PVA, PVA/ONFC, and PVA-ONFC@CD PF films were comparatively evaluated by TGA, LOI, vertical combustion (UL-94) and micro-scale heat release measurements. Table 1 presents the detailed data. The TGA curves of films are presented in Fig. 9a. All samples showed a slight mass loss below 180 °C owing to the evaporation of moisture and low-weight molecules. Two degradation stages primarily occurred in all films. For the PVA and PVA-ONFC composite films, severe losses occurred between 250 and 380 °C, which was attributed to the degradation of ONFC and the side chain of PVA [50]. The second stage was observed at 400–510 °C, which was attributed to the PVA breakage and cyclization, respectively [51,52]. For the films with dots, the T1 temperature gradually decreased with an increasing ratio of dots (Table S5). This is because that abundant groups of N,S,P-CD PF were eliminated at 220–250 °C. The incorporation of dots led to a sharp increase in the number of side groups in the films, thereby promoting the pyrolysis of the matrix. In the second stage at 400–510 °C, the peak of pyrolysis for all films was similar. Moreover, the fluorescent films exhibited an evident increase in carbon residuals (25.50 wt% of PVA-ONFC@CD PF10 ) up to 800 °C. Such good thermal stability can protect blocking films from high temperature fluctuations in practical applications.

Fig. 9b presents the heat-release rate (HRR) curves of PVA-based films, and Table 1 lists the typical results from the HRR curves. The pristine PVA and PVA/ONFC films burn violently, and the peak of HRR (PHRR) value of PVA film reached 287.4 and 257.6 W/g at 338.5 °C and 347.2 °C, respectively. Evidently, the HRR value of the composite films was obviously decreased with an increase ratio of the N,S,P-CD PF , and the total heat release (THR) value also decreased from 20.9 to 8.5 KJ/g for the PVA-ONFC@CD PF5 film (0.05 wt% N,S,P-CD PF ) compared to the control PVA film. The temperature at PHRR for the PVA-ONFC@CD PF5 film also reduced to 274.5 °C, which indicates that the integration of PF-derived N,S,P-CD PF can effectively delay and reduce the HRR of PVA-based films while enhancing the flame retardancy. It mainly ascribed to the abundant N-, P-, S-containing compounds in the PF-derived N,S,P-CD PF , which effectively promotes the dehydration carbonization of matrix to form a dense char layer and restrain the fire propagation.

Fig. 9c–f show the combustion images captured from the camera. The PVA and PVA/ONFC films burned violently and showed almost no residue after 6–8 s combustion, as shown in Fig. 8c and d. The droplet behavior of PVA is more serious than that of PVA/ONFC film after ignition. It mainly contributed to the increased carbon-forming behavior of the ONFC integration. Moreover, the LOI for the PVA and PVA/ONFC film was only 19.2 % and 19.8 %, respectively (Table 1). Upon adding 0.01 wt% N,S,P-CD PF , the droplet behavior of PVA-ONFC@CD PF1 disappeared, and the LOI increased obviously to 25.2 %. The PVA-ONFC@CD PF1 film was slowly burned after continuous ignition for 2 s, the fire on the bottom of the film was quickly extinguished after the fire source was removed. Particularly, the PVA-ONFC@CD PF5 film only slightly twisted and shrinked without burn even under continuous fire source. The PVA-ONFC@CD PF5 and PVA-ONFC@CD PF10 films successfully reached the UL-94 VTM-0 rating with the LOI value of 31.7 % and 33.4 %, respectively. Thus, the addition of >0.05 wt% N,S,P-CD PF can enhance the flame retardancy of films. It is worth mentioning that the N,S,P-CD PF addition content was extremely low compared to the traditional flame retardants, but they present effective flame retardant properties for PVA-based films.

To summarize, the facile synthesis of dual-functional N,S,P-CD PF with a QY of up to 12.6 % have been presented by recycling thermosetting phenolic foam waste. The fabricated N,S,P-CD PF have displayed good graphite structure with rich N-, S-, P-, and C-doped groups and presented admirable UV absorption and flame retardant properties. The absorption peak and fluorescent QY of N,S,P-CD PF were affected by the hydrothermal precursors and reaction. The N,S,P-CD PF were then incorporated into PVA/ONFC polymer to create a flexible and dual-functional film via the solution casting approach. The light-blocking efficiency and flame retardancy of composite films were verified by adjusting the loading ratio of N,S,P-CD PF in films. The integration of ONFC remarkably improved the mechanical strength of composite films. The film with 0.05 wt% N,S,P-CD PF displayed excellent tensile strength of 55.8 MPa, good flame retardancy (VTM-0 level), 76.5 % transparency at 800 nm, good ability to block most of UV light and an additional ∼39.6 % of HEBL at 450 nm. Another outcome is that the PF-derived N,S,P-CD PF have proved to be good flame retardants owing to the increased LOI (31.7 %) and reduced HRR (from 287.4 W/g to 127.2 W/g) values with extremely low addition content (0.05 wt% N,S,P-CD PF ) compared to the PVA film. Finally, this work offers a promising idea for recycling of thermosetting PF waste as a flame retardant UV absorber, and provides a solution for the UV-blocking and flame retardant packaging materials.

Longfei Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing the original draft, Visualization. Limin Peng: Validation, Resources, Formal analysis. Shanqing Liang: Formal analysis. Xinfeng Xie: Validation, Review. Shaoyi Lyu: Supervision, Project administration, Editing. Siqun Wang: Validation, Review, Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32001261), the basic fund for central level public welfare research institutes (CAFYBB2022MB001), the USDA Multi-State project (S-1075), and the Open Fund of Key Laboratory (2019KFJJ13).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.porgcoat.2023.107872.
